full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Many of you are familiar with the catechumenate program here at St. John's.<|6.60|> <|7.00|> It is the series of classes comprised of brief teachings and small group discussion<|14.94|> <|14.94|> that get folks ready for a variety of ways to enter life at St. John's and the church at large.<|24.14|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Whether folks just want to know a bit more about us or the wider Episcopal Church, or they're really called to participate in a sacramental rite, baptism, confirmation, reception, reaffirmation of their baptismal vows,<|21.70|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> All of those happen at the great vigil of Easter, which is spectacular with the bishop present.<|7.14|> <|8.84|> Catechumenate is a time of questioning and deep theological conversation, community building, and formation.<|18.52|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> St. John's has held a catechumenate course for decades.<|5.60|> <|5.60|> It started in the 1990s under Dean Kiblinger, and many of the priests who have served here<|13.96|> <|13.96|> in the intervening years have taken this very model with them to the parishes they've moved<|19.76|> <|19.76|> on to serve.<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Catechumenate is an ancient way of forming Christians, and here at St. John's it is a<|10.24|> <|10.24|> time-honored tradition, and it is proven to be the fertile soil producing many lasting<|16.90|> <|16.90|> friendships and strong lay leaders for this community.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.68|> Richard and I have been tweaking the structure<|4.34|> <|4.34|> a little bit this year.<|6.74|> <|6.74|> We will not be starting the formal catechumenate until January,<|11.74|> <|11.74|> so there is still time.<|13.34|> <|15.28|> Please join us if any of that sounded interesting to you.<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We've met a few times with folks since September to discuss a book we are using, one Broderick<|8.08|> <|8.08|> introduced us to, a people called Episcopalians.<|13.94|> <|13.94|> It's a concise yet detailed look at what we believe and some of the aspects of our tradition<|23.20|> <|23.20|> that make us unique.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.46|> Just before the holidays, we gathered<|4.44|> <|4.44|> to explore what's been my favorite chapter so far,<|8.48|> <|8.48|> temperament.<|9.50|> <|10.76|> Who are we as a people?<|14.56|> <|14.56|> How do we move through the world?<|16.88|> <|16.88|> And how do we encounter both the sacred and the secular<|22.00|> <|22.00|> through our particular Episcopal lens?<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It takes into account multiple topics, including intuition, aesthetics, politics, ambiguity, and more.<|11.72|> <|13.00|> And some of those are not mutually exclusive.<|14.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.10|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The point we kept returning to in our discussion of temperament was our foundation in the via media, the middle way.<|11.84|> <|13.14|> Early Anglican theologians embraced this notion of moderation between the Protestant reformers and Catholicism.<|24.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Moderation was viewed as a strength over the inherent weaknesses of extremism.<|8.54|> <|10.74|> Episcopalians still seek out that middle ground.<|15.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and find strength and virtue in holding two seemingly opposing things in tension.<|8.06|> <|8.92|> Recognizing that two things can be true at once.<|15.04|> <|16.24|> Holding that tension between the secular and the sacred.<|20.86|> <|23.04|> Advent calls us to do this very thing.<|27.10|> <|27.10|> Amen.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> to explore and live in this middle ground.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> On the one hand, we face the practicalities and realities of the looming Christmas season.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> Richard reminded us last Sunday that we will have barely finished cleaning up the Thanksgiving dishes<|22.00|> <|22.00|> before we're planning our Christmas meal.<|26.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And on the other hand, we want to embrace the contemplative and quiet anticipation that<|11.06|> <|11.06|> is Advent.<|13.94|> <|13.94|> Thoughts of Jesus' return, the vigilance of preparation, the anticipation of the coming<|21.38|> <|21.38|> of the light of the world.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We have candle lighting, we have special prayers, and we have worship, like our service of healing and wholeness, this Wednesday in the chapel.<|13.82|> <|15.28|> As Episcopalians, we are better suited by our temperament, I would think, than many to hold these two realities in tension.<|27.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We've got a good and healthy dose of both Martha and Mary in us.<|7.10|> <|7.10|> The attention to hosting and preparing balanced with moments of sitting at Jesus' feet.<|16.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the gospel lesson for today, our first foray into our year of Matthew, hold on to your hats,<|9.42|> <|10.44|> we hear Jesus say no fewer than five times, about that day and hour, no one knows.<|20.56|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If the angels in heaven and the Son of Man himself do not know the day nor the hour of his return,<|9.56|> <|10.44|> we cannot expect to know.<|12.78|> <|13.70|> We are simply to be prepared.<|16.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Just as the people were eating and drinking and marrying in the days of Noah,<|7.00|> <|7.00|> and just as laborers are working in the field and women were grinding meal,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> we are also to keep doing the work of living while we wait.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.44|> If we were to sit up every night,<|5.38|> <|5.38|> keeping vigil over our home,<|8.78|> <|8.78|> watching our doors and being hyper attentive<|12.00|> <|12.00|> to every creak and groan of a floorboard<|16.38|> <|16.38|> while waiting for that possible thief to enter,<|20.20|> <|21.26|> we're going to find ourselves<|23.76|> <|23.76|> quickly incapable of functioning.<|26.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.50|> This level of alertness is unsustainable.<|7.20|> <|9.10|> Instead, we might have deadbolts on our door.<|12.80|> <|12.80|> We might have outside lights to illuminate vulnerable areas.<|17.80|> <|19.16|> We may even have an alarm system.<|21.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We are vigilant and wise, but we are not paranoid and paralyzed.<|8.88|> <|9.88|> We hold intention, the practical, with the preparation.<|15.26|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|0.18|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As a young mother of three small children, I would sometimes find myself lost in the<|9.86|> <|9.86|> midst of the fourth load of laundry that day, or picking up toys for the umpteenth time<|18.36|> <|18.36|> that day, or doing some other repetitive task.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I love my children, don't mistake me, but I would often find myself thinking that my college degree was looking less and less useful by the moment.<|12.16|> <|14.10|> How to find joy, purpose, and inspiration in the midst of the mundane daily tasks required of stay-at-home motherhood?<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I was definitely struggling at times.<|3.22|> <|4.00|> And a dear friend and spiritual mentor shared with me a small book that I have grown to love,<|11.02|> <|11.76|> The Practice of the Presence of God by Brother Lawrence.<|15.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Brother Lawrence was not a stay-at-home mom, but he was a 17th century French Carmelite monk<|9.00|> <|9.00|> who developed a simple way of praying from his heart, which he called "practicing the presence of God."<|17.00|> <|17.00|> And he learned to commune with God continuously, not only in church or personal devotions, but also as he worked.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> in the kitchen, as he ran errands or listened to people.<|5.44|> <|6.46|> In his own words, Brother Lawrence said,<|9.12|> <|9.12|> The time of business does not differ for me with the time of prayer.<|16.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And in the noise and clatter of my kitchen, while several persons are at the same time calling for different things,<|7.44|> <|7.44|> I possess God in as great a tranquility as if I were upon my knees at the blessed sacrament.<|16.36|> <|17.70|> So yes, laundry can be holy when we invite God into it.<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.06|> Sounds a lot like Advent, doesn't it?<|4.48|> <|5.38|> An embodiment of that Martha and Mary tension<|9.04|> <|9.04|> in the midst of the reality of our over-busyness in December.<|14.16|> <|15.36|> Take time to acknowledge the balancing act<|19.44|> <|19.44|> and invite God in.<|22.10|> <|23.14|> That is vital.<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> vigilance.<|0.68|> <|1.88|> You choosing a hostess gift<|4.34|> <|4.34|> for a holiday party?<|5.50|> <|6.50|> Say a prayer of thanksgiving<|8.28|> <|8.28|> for the blessing<|9.78|> <|9.78|> of good friends and the<|12.16|> <|12.16|> opportunity for fellowship.<|13.30|> <|14.36|> Are you wrapping gifts?<|16.20|> <|17.76|> Pray for<|18.30|> all.<|1.26|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Or perhaps you are feeling lonely, as so many do, in the midst of so much forced celebration.<|11.60|> <|12.96|> Pour out <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Advent and Christmas need not be incompatible, my friends.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> I bristle at those who would make us feel guilty.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> You need not feel guilty about attending to the practical and the spiritual.<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the via media, that middle way of moderation and a balanced life is what we are called to,<|9.50|> <|10.54|> preparing both outwardly and inwardly, and always holding in tension the reality of our present<|19.70|> <|19.70|> and looking toward our hope.<|22.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
